text: The app is not useful anymore after the last update.
rationale: This uses negation before a positive cue, useful, to test whether the model reads not useful as negative.

text: I do not recommend this app even though the design looks clean.
rationale: This tests whether the model catches do not recommend instead of focusing on the positive design cue.

text: The update did not improve performance at all.
rationale: This negates the positive word improve to test whether the model relies only on the cue word.

text: The app is not bad, but it is still missing basic features.
rationale: This has a negated negative phrase with a mixed follow-up, so the intended label is not strongly positive or negative.

text: I cannot say this app is amazing because it crashes too often.
rationale: This tests whether the model ignores cannot and reacts to amazing.

text: The interface looks beautiful, but the app deletes my saved work.
rationale: This contains the positive trigger beautiful, but the real experience is negative.

text: Reliable was what this app used to be; now it freezes every day.
rationale: This flips the positive cue reliable by putting it in the past and contrasting it with current failure.

text: Great idea, terrible execution.
rationale: This starts with a positive trigger but ends with a stronger negative judgment.

text: I love the colors, but I cannot complete a single payment.
rationale: This tests whether the model overweights love and ignores the serious functional failure.

text: The app is fast and polished, but I do not need it.
rationale: This includes positive surface words, but the overall review is more neutral than positive.

text: The company reported higher revenue after cutting operating costs.
rationale: This is business news prose, not a user opinion, so sentiment should be neutral.

text: The football team lost the final after a penalty shootout.
rationale: This sports sentence contains negative event language but is factual reporting.

text: Bake the cake for thirty minutes and let it cool before serving.
rationale: This recipe instruction has no app-review sentiment.

text: The senator criticized the new policy during a press conference.
rationale: This news-style sentence uses criticized, but it reports an event rather than a user sentiment.

text: The movie opened at number one during the holiday weekend.
rationale: This entertainment news sentence sounds positive but is mainly factual.

text: Works fine.
rationale: This very short review tests whether the model can classify minimal positive feedback.

text: Still broken.
rationale: This very short review tests whether the model catches a minimal negative complaint.

text: Okay, nothing special.
rationale: This short sentence is mild and should be neutral.

text: After using this app for three weeks, I like the clean layout, the quick search, and the simple setup, but I still find the notifications confusing, the settings menu hard to navigate, and the sync feature unreliable when switching between my laptop and phone.
rationale: This long mixed review tests whether the model can balance positive and negative evidence.

text: I downloaded the app because a friend recommended it, and at first the onboarding felt smooth, but after several days the login screen started looping, customer support sent generic replies, and the features I needed were locked behind a confusing upgrade page.
rationale: This long review contains mixed context but ends with several concrete negative failures.

text: Fantastic, another update that makes the app crash faster.
rationale: This uses positive surface wording sarcastically to express a negative experience.

text: Amazing job hiding the only button I use every day.
rationale: This sarcastically praises a design change that actually made the app worse.

text: Love paying for premium and still seeing ads.
rationale: This uses love sarcastically with a complaint about ads.

text: Perfect, the app logged me out right before my meeting.
rationale: This uses a positive word, perfect, to express frustration.

text: Great, now the app needs permission for everything just to open.
rationale: This tests whether the model reads great literally instead of detecting sarcastic complaint.

text: The app is useful for beginners, but advanced users may outgrow it quickly.
rationale: This mixed-audience case tests whether the model can handle conditional sentiment rather than one clear opinion.

text: I would rate it five stars if the export button worked.
rationale: This counterfactual sentence contains a positive rating cue but the actual condition is unmet.

text: This is not a bad app for a free tool.
rationale: This tests a softened positive judgment using negated negative wording.

text: The app does exactly what it promises and nothing more.
rationale: This checks whether plain adequacy is treated as neutral rather than positive.

text: I wanted to hate it, but it actually saved me a lot of time.
rationale: This starts negative but flips to positive, testing contrast handling.
